title: Numbers & Money
description: Counting, prices, bargaining — the practical math of Delhi life.
culturalNote: Hindi numbers are irregular and you just have to learn them. But the good news: in real Delhi life, people use English numbers for prices constantly. 'Five hundred rupees' is as normal as 'paanch sau'. What you DO need is the bargaining phrases — those always stay in Hindi.

exercises.note: Teen numbers are irregular — memorise them as a group. Gyarah (11), barah (12), terah (13), chaudah (14), pandrah (15).
Solah (16), satrah (17), atharah (18), unnees (19), bees (20). 'Bees' for twenty is easy to remember.

exercises.hint: Bees = 20 — sounds a bit like 'bees (as in bees buzzing)'

exercises.note: Tees (30), chaalees (40), pachaas (50), saath (60), sattar (70), assi (80), nabbe (90). The round tens are very commonly used in prices.
'Ek sau' or just 'sau' = 100. 'Do sau' = 200. 'Paanch sau' = 500. 'Sau' is the backbone of pricing — you'll hear it everywhere.
'Ek hazaar' = 1,000. 'Do hazaar' = 2,000. 'Paanch hazaar' = 5,000. For rent, phone bills, travel — hazaar is your unit.

exercises.prompt: The auto driver says 'do sau rupaye'. How much is he asking for?

exercises.note: India uses lakh, not 'hundred thousand'. 1 lakh = 1,00,000. Salaries, rents, car prices are in lakhs. '5 lakh ka ghar' = a 5 lakh house (~$6,000). Essential for real-world finance talk.
1 crore = 10 million. Big business, movie budgets, expensive properties. '50 crore ki film' = a 50 crore film. The Indian number system: 1 → 10 → 100 → 1,000 → 1 lakh → 10 lakh → 1 crore.

exercises.prompt: Your friend says their new apartment costs '50 lakh'. How much is that?

exercises.english: How much is it? / What does it cost?
exercises.note: Your first question in any market or shop. 'Kitne ka?' alone is slightly more casual. 'Kitne mein milega?' = how much will I get it for? (slightly more negotiation-ready)

exercises.english: How much does it come to? / What's the total?
exercises.note: Use at restaurants and after buying multiple items. 'Kitna hua total?' = what's the total? Slightly different from 'kitne ka hai?' — this asks for a final amount, not a price tag.

exercises.english: Reduce it a little, won't you? / Give a discount
exercises.note: The core bargaining phrase. 'Na' at the end makes it a polite request, not a demand. 'Thoda' = a little (not too aggressive). Works in markets, autos, street food. Essential for Delhi shopping.

exercises.sentence: Bhaiya, sau rupaye bahut zyada hai. _____.
exercises.hint: Asking for a reduction — polite bargaining phrase

exercises.english: Won't I get anything off? / No discount at all?
exercises.note: Slightly more insistent than 'thoda kam karo na'. 'Kuch' = something/anything. Use this after the first no. Body language matters — smile while saying it.

exercises.english: Is this the fixed price? / Is this final?
exercises.note: 'Pakka' = fixed/sure/certain. In fixed-price shops you'd ask to confirm. In markets it's a setup for negotiation. 'Pakka' alone = it's for sure / confirmed.

exercises.prompt: The shopkeeper says '300 rupaye'. You want to negotiate. What do you say first?

exercises.sentenceEnglish: Brother, how much will you take for the auto?
exercises.hint: How much — the question word for price
